q: A hydraulic press can lift <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> when a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is placed on the smaller piston. It can lift kg when the diameter of the larger piston is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times and that of the smaller piston is decreased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times keeping the same mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> on the smaller piston.
question: A hydraulic press can lift <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> when a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is placed on the smaller piston. It can lift kg when the diameter of the larger piston is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times and that of the smaller piston is decreased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times keeping the same mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> on the smaller piston.
solution:
Using Pascals law, 

100×gA2=mgA1         ...1

Let m mass can lift M0 in second case then

M0g16A2=mgA1/16          ...2

Since A=πd24

From equation 1 and 2 we get

M016.100=16

⇒M0=25600 kg

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment to verify Stokes law, a small spherical ball of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> falls under gravity through a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> in air before entering a tank of water. If the terminal velocity of the ball inside water is same as its velocity just before entering the water surface, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> is proportional to: (ignore viscosity of air)
question: In an experiment to verify Stokes law, a small spherical ball of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> and density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ρ</mi></math> falls under gravity through a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> in air before entering a tank of water. If the terminal velocity of the ball inside water is same as its velocity just before entering the water surface, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> is proportional to: (ignore viscosity of air)
solution:
The velocity of ball just before striking the surface of water is given by using kinematical equation of motion under the gravity

v2=u2-2gy

⇒v2=-2g(-h)

⇒v=2gh  ...(1) 

Now, the terminal velocity of spherical ball inside the water tank is given by,

vT=29r2(ρ-σ)gη  ...(2)

After falling through h the velocity of ball should be equal to its terminal velocity.

From equation (1) and (2) we get,

⇒2gh=29r2(ρ-σ)gη

Squaring the above equation, we get

⇒h=281r4g(ρ-σ)2η2

⇒h∝r4

subject: Physics
q: A spherical liquid drop of radius $R$ acquires the terminal velocity $v_1$ when falls through a gas of viscosity $\eta$. Now the drop is broken into $64$ identical droplets and each droplet acquires terminal velocity $v_2$ falling through the same gas. The ratio of terminal velocities $v_1/v_2$ is ________.
question: A spherical liquid drop of radius $R$ acquires the terminal velocity $v_1$ when falls through a gas of viscosity $\eta$. Now the drop is broken into $64$ identical droplets and each droplet acquires terminal velocity $v_2$ falling through the same gas. The ratio of terminal velocities $v_1/v_2$ is ________.
solution:
The terminal velocity of a spherical drop of radius $r$ falling through a viscous medium is given by Stokes' law:

$v = \dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^2 (\rho - \sigma) g}{\eta}$

From this relation, the terminal velocity is directly proportional to the square of the radius of the drop:

$v \propto r^2$

When the large drop of radius $R$ is broken into $64$ identical droplets of radius $r$, the total volume remains conserved:

$\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^3 = 64 \times \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$

$R^3 = 64 r^3$

$R = 4r$

The ratio of the terminal velocities of the original drop and the smaller droplet is:

$\dfrac{v_1}{v_2} = \dfrac{R^2}{r^2}$

Substituting $R = 4r$:

$\dfrac{v_1}{v_2} = \dfrac{(4r)^2}{r^2} = 16$

Answer: $16$

subject: Physics
q: If an air bubble of diameter $2$ mm rises steadily through a liquid of density $2000$ kg/m$^3$ at a rate of $0.5$ cm/s, then the coefficient of viscosity of liquid is _______ Poise. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question: If an air bubble of diameter $2$ mm rises steadily through a liquid of density $2000$ kg/m$^3$ at a rate of $0.5$ cm/s, then the coefficient of viscosity of liquid is _______ Poise. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
The terminal velocity of an air bubble rising in a liquid is given by Stokes' law:

$v = \dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^2 \rho g}{\eta}$

Where:
$r = \dfrac{D}{2} = 1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
$\rho = 2000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
$g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$
$v = 0.5 \text{ cm/s} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$

Rearranging the formula for the coefficient of viscosity $\eta$:

$\eta = \dfrac{2}{9} \dfrac{r^2 \rho g}{v}$

Substituting the given values:

$\eta = \dfrac{2}{9} \times \dfrac{(10^{-3})^2 \times 2000 \times 10}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$

$\eta = \dfrac{2}{9} \times \dfrac{10^{-6} \times 20000}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$

$\eta = \dfrac{2}{9} \times \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$

$\eta = \dfrac{2}{9} \times 4 = \dfrac{8}{9} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$

To convert the viscosity from SI units (Pa$\cdot$s) to CGS units (Poise), we multiply by $10$:

$\eta = \dfrac{8}{9} \times 10 = \dfrac{80}{9} \text{ Poise}$

$\eta \approx 8.88 \text{ Poise}$

The closest given option is $8.8$.

Answer: $8.8$

subject: Physics
q: The terminal velocity of a metallic ball of radius 6 mm in a viscous fluid is $20 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. The terminal velocity of another ball of same material and having radius 3 mm in the same fluid will be $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: The terminal velocity of a metallic ball of radius 6 mm in a viscous fluid is $20 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$. The terminal velocity of another ball of same material and having radius 3 mm in the same fluid will be $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
For a sphere in a viscous fluid, terminal velocity is proportional to $r^2$: $v_t \propto r^2$.
For two balls of the same material in the same fluid: $\frac{v_{t1}}{v_{t2}} = \frac{r_1^2}{r_2^2}$.
Given $r_1 = 6$ mm, $v_{t1} = 20$ cm/s, and $r_2 = 3$ mm: $\frac{20}{v_{t2}} = \frac{36}{9} = 4$.
Therefore, $v_{t2} = 5$ cm/s.

q:
A spherical ball of radius 1 mm and density 10.5 g cc-1 is dropped in glycerine of coefficient of viscosity 9.8 poise and density 1.5 g cc-1. Viscous force on the ball when it attains constant velocity is 3696×10-x N. The value of x is

(Given, g=9.8 m s-2 and π=227)
question:
A spherical ball of radius 1 mm and density 10.5 g cc-1 is dropped in glycerine of coefficient of viscosity 9.8 poise and density 1.5 g cc-1. Viscous force on the ball when it attains constant velocity is 3696×10-x N. The value of x is

(Given, g=9.8 m s-2 and π=227)
solution:
Viscous force on the ball when it attains constant velocity i.e., terminal velocity is,

 Fv=mg-FB  As, a=0

Here, FB is buoyant force.

⇒Fv=Vρbg-Vρgg=Vgρb-ρg=43π10-33×9.810.5-1.5×103≈3696×10-7 N

Thus, x=7.

q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: The hot water flows faster than cold water
Statement II: Soap water has higher surface tension as compared to fresh water.
In the light above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: The hot water flows faster than cold water
Statement II: Soap water has higher surface tension as compared to fresh water.
In the light above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution:
Hot water is less viscous then cold water.
Surfactant reduces surface tension.

subject: Physics
q: A cubical block of density $\rho_{b}=600 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ floats in a liquid of density $\rho_{\mathrm{e}}=900 \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$. If the height of block is $H=8.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ then height of the submerged part is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
question: A cubical block of density $\rho_{b}=600 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$ floats in a liquid of density $\rho_{\mathrm{e}}=900 \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$. If the height of block is $H=8.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ then height of the submerged part is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
solution:
For a floating cubical block in equilibrium, the buoyant force equals the weight:
$\rho_e \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h_s = \rho_b \cdot g \cdot A \cdot H$
where $h_s$ is the submerged height.
Simplifying: $h_s = \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_e} \cdot H = \frac{600}{900} \times 8.0 = \frac{2}{3} \times 8.0$
$= 5.33$ cm ≈ 5.3 cm

Let <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> be the pressure inside the inner bubble and <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math>, the pressure outside the outer bubble. Radius of another bubble with pressure difference <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> between its inside and outside would be:
question: A small soap bubble of radius 4cm is trapped inside another bubble of radius 6cm without any contact. Let <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> be the pressure inside the inner bubble and <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math>, the pressure outside the outer bubble. Radius of another bubble with pressure difference <math><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> between its inside and outside would be:
solution:
P2−P1=4Tr1      ....(1)P1−P0=4Tr2  .....(2)adding (1) & (2)P2−P0=4Tr1+4Tr2P2=P0+4Tr1+4Tr2P2=P0+4T6+4T4

for another bubble of radius r

P2= P0+4Tr

⇒   1r=16+14

⇒   r=2.4 cm

q: Water from a pipe is coming at a rate of <math><mn>100</mn></math> liters per minute. If the radius of the pipe is <math><mn>5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the Reynolds number for the flow is of the order of: (density of water = <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> coefficient of viscosity of water <math><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>)</mo></math>
question: Water from a pipe is coming at a rate of <math><mn>100</mn></math> liters per minute. If the radius of the pipe is <math><mn>5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the Reynolds number for the flow is of the order of: (density of water = <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> coefficient of viscosity of water <math><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
Volume flow rate =πr2v=100 lmin
⇒πr2v=1600 m3/sec
⇒v=1600 πr2 m/sec
Reynolds number Re=ρvDη
=10001×10-3×1600 πr2×2r
=20000.6π5×10-2=21220.66=2.1×104

subject: Physics
q:
The velocity of a small ball of mass 0.3 g and density 8 g cc-1 when dropped in a container filled with glycerine becomes constant after some time. If the density of glycerine is 1.3 g cc-1, then the value of viscous force acting on the ball will be x×10-4 N, the value of x is _____ .

[use g=10 m s-2]
question:
The velocity of a small ball of mass 0.3 g and density 8 g cc-1 when dropped in a container filled with glycerine becomes constant after some time. If the density of glycerine is 1.3 g cc-1, then the value of viscous force acting on the ball will be x×10-4 N, the value of x is _____ .

[use g=10 m s-2]
solution:
Let FV be the viscous force and FB be the buoyant force acting on the ball.

​​​​​​​

Then, when body moves with constant velocity,

FV+FB=Mg   

Or, FV=Mg-FB

FV=ρBVg-ρLVg =ρB-ρLVg, where, V is the volume of small ball.

=8-1.3×103×0.3×10-38×103×10

=6.7×0.38×10-2

=67×38×10-4=25.125×10-4=25×10-4 N

Thus, the value of x=25.
